caveats: a: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated AI task exposure for NAICS 327310.
a: Existing process-control automation may make generic manufacturing AI studies overstate incremental exposure.
rho: Demonstration and award announcements do not establish scaled adoption or realized labor savings.
rho: Implementation varies sharply with plant age, control-system vendor, data quality, and union or safety constraints.
e: The frozen firm-count estimate may be misleading because plant-scale cement economics and product manufacturing do not align well with the service-company lens.
e: Classification is establishment-based, whereas eligibility is assessed at the firm and contract level.
s5: This is not an observed deal rate.
s5: The estimate is conditional on eligibility; because eligible firms may be near zero, even one transaction would move the rate substantially.
q: No direct estimate of five-year pass-through for AI-generated cement cost savings was found.
q: Regional capacity and transport radii can make retention differ substantially by plant.
d5: The sources report tons or consumption, not a constant-quality service-basket index.
d5: Interest rates, public infrastructure timing, imports, and substitution by blended products create substantial cyclical and mix uncertainty.
o: This assesses operator requirement for the physical manufacturing basket, even though almost none of that basket qualifies as an outsourced service under the lens.
o: Remote operations could reduce on-site labor without eliminating the accountable operating firm.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The underlying industry predominantly sells a physical commodity-like product to ready-mix concrete producers and other construction-material customers; only separately contracted recurring external services would qualify for the screen.
lens.excluded_activities: Ordinary sales of cement and clinker, captive internal service units, mines and quarries classified elsewhere, ready-mix concrete producers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. cement manufacturers that manufacture hydraulic cement and also have a genuinely recurring or repeat outsourced operating-service relationship with external customers, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated AI task exposure for NAICS 327310.
Existing process-control automation may make generic manufacturing AI studies overstate incremental exposure.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit OEWS staffing-and-wage file joined to O*NET task statements, plus time-and-motion studies from U.S. cement plants separating automated control-room work from field work, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Cement production is a physical continuous process, so most plant, maintenance, material-handling, and safety work is not substitutable by current AI. The exposed slice is concentrated in process monitoring, predictive-maintenance analysis, quality documentation, scheduling, procurement, sales administration, and emissions reporting. DOE identifies AI, machine learning, robotics, sensors, analytics, and controls as relevant to energy-intensive manufacturing including cement, but does not publish a wage-weighted cement task share.

caveats: The sources report tons or consumption, not a constant-quality service-basket index.
Interest rates, public infrastructure timing, imports, and substitution by blended products create substantial cyclical and mix uncertainty.
d5.change_evidence: ACA's detailed national forecast, Census construction-put-in-place scenarios, infrastructure award schedules, and region-level cement shipment forecasts through 2031 would materially change this estimate.
d5.rationale: Cement remains a core construction input. USGS estimated 2025 apparent consumption at 110 million metric tons, unchanged from 2024, while ACA forecast a 2.5% decline in 2026 followed by a return to positive growth in 2027. Those observations support broadly flat constant-quality five-year demand with a cyclical range around the base.

caveats: The frozen firm-count estimate may be misleading because plant-scale cement economics and product manufacturing do not align well with the service-company lens.
Classification is establishment-based, whereas eligibility is assessed at the firm and contract level.
e.change_evidence: Revenue-segment data and customer contracts for every LMM-band firm, explicitly identifying separately priced recurring external services, could establish whether any firms actually qualify.
e.rationale: The Census definition is manufacturing hydraulic cement, while the screen requires recurring outsourced services to external customers. Ordinary cement sales do not qualify. USGS reports that 70% to 75% of cement sales go to ready-mixed concrete producers, reinforcing a product-sales model. A very small allowance remains for manufacturers with separately contracted qualifying service revenue that is still classified in 327310.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 327310 ($1,494,194,569) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 327310 ($11,446,208,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $818,873 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 327310); class <5: 21 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,289,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $188,887 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,249,067 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $329,488 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,907,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,011,875 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,614,308 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,554,996 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $43,148,833 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,321,304 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $561,808,842 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $82,304,995 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 33

caveats: This assesses operator requirement for the physical manufacturing basket, even though almost none of that basket qualifies as an outsourced service under the lens.
Remote operations could reduce on-site labor without eliminating the accountable operating firm.
o.change_evidence: Evidence from fully autonomous commercial cement plants, including staffing, remote-operation scope, regulatory acceptance, and third-party self-service substitution, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Year-five cement demand still requires physical quarrying or input receipt, kiln operation, grinding, testing, loading, maintenance, safety management, and environmental accountability. Software can improve controls and documentation but cannot itself supply cement, and EPA rules assign monitoring and reporting duties to facility owners or operators.

caveats: No direct estimate of five-year pass-through for AI-generated cement cost savings was found.
Regional capacity and transport radii can make retention differ substantially by plant.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract samples showing escalation clauses, bid frequency, freight terms, rebates, and realized gross-margin persistence following plant productivity projects would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Cement is sold as a standardized physical input to concentrated downstream categories, so productivity benefits can be competed away through unit-price negotiations and imports. Some benefit can persist through local freight economics, capacity constraints, and proprietary operating execution, but multi-year retention is unlikely to resemble a sticky fixed-fee service contract.

caveats: Demonstration and award announcements do not establish scaled adoption or realized labor savings.
Implementation varies sharply with plant age, control-system vendor, data quality, and union or safety constraints.
rho.change_evidence: Plant-level deployment data from cement producers showing AI use cases, production coverage, realized labor hours, exception rates, and audit outcomes over at least two years would materially narrow this estimate.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be meaningful in bounded analytics and administrative workflows, but integration with legacy controls, high uptime requirements, emissions compliance, cyber risk, and the need for accountable operators constrain deployment. DOE documents smart-manufacturing demonstrations in cement, while EPA requirements show that regulated monitoring and reporting remain operator obligations.

caveats: This is not an observed deal rate.
The estimate is conditional on eligibility; because eligible firms may be near zero, even one transaction would move the rate substantially.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, and state permit-change records, matched to qualifying U.S. firms and distinguishing control sales from internal reorganizations, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: No industry-specific control-transfer series was found for the tiny qualifying subset. Large fixed assets, environmental liabilities, permitting, and strategic-buyer concentration can lengthen transactions, while ordinary succession and sponsor holding periods still create some five-year transfer probability.

principal_driver: Durable physical construction demand and bounded AI opportunities in process, maintenance, and compliance workflows.
principal_weakness: The industry sells cement rather than a recurring outsourced service, so eligible firm share is likely near zero.
